content.text: Ollama is a tool that makes running open-source LLMs locally as simple as running a Docker container. A single command downloads a model, stores it in a local library, starts a background inference process, and serves it through a REST API indistinguishable from OpenAI's. It abstracts over llama.cpp, manages the model lifecycle, and integrates with the same Python clients, LangChain pipelines, and embedding stores that developers already use against the OpenAI API.

Ollama is a local LLM runtime composed of three parts: a background daemon process, a command-line interface, and an HTTP REST API. The daemon — launched automatically when you first run any ollama command — loads models on demand, manages the KV cache, and serves completions through port 11434. The CLI provides human-friendly commands for pulling models, running interactive sessions, listing installed models, and managing Modelfiles. The REST API exposes endpoints compatible with both Ollama's own format and the OpenAI API format, so existing client code and LLM frameworks work without modification.
as its inference backend. Models are stored in the GGUF format and loaded by llama.cpp's runtime. Ollama contributes the management layer: a model registry with named versions (the

naming convention), automatic hardware detection, GPU acceleration on macOS via Metal and on NVIDIA hardware via CUDA, and a clean process model that keeps a loaded model in memory across multiple requests rather than reloading weights for every call.

Before Ollama, running a local LLM meant manually downloading GGUF files, building llama.cpp from source, crafting the correct command-line invocation with GPU layer counts and context sizes, and writing a custom server wrapper. Ollama reduces that process to two commands: ollama pull followed by ollama run. The model is cached locally and served instantly on subsequent requests. This reduction in friction is significant — it lets developers integrate a local model into a prototype in the same time it would take to sign up for an API key.

The cost and privacy dimensions are equally important. Because every token is generated locally, there are no per-call costs and no data leaves the machine. For applications that process personal health records, confidential legal documents, proprietary source code, or any data subject to regulatory constraints, Ollama provides a path to useful LLM capabilities without requiring a cloud API arrangement or a legal review of a vendor's data retention policies.
ollama pull downloads and verifies a GGUF model from the Ollama library registry in one command, with no manual file handling.
Ollama detects and uses Metal on macOS Apple Silicon and CUDA on NVIDIA GPUs automatically, with no configuration flags required.
the model stays loaded in memory between requests, eliminating the 5–30 second cold-start penalty that would otherwise accompany every API call.
Ollama's /v1/chat/completions endpoint accepts the same request format as the OpenAI API. Existing code needs only a changed base_url to point at the local server.
a Modelfile (analogous to a Dockerfile) lets you set system prompts, sampling parameters, and the base model in a versioned text file, producing a named local model variant.
the Ollama library hosts Llama 3, Mistral, Phi-3, Qwen 2, Gemma 2, DeepSeek, Code Llama, and dozens of other open-source models, each in multiple quantization variants.
first-class support in LangChain, LlamaIndex, Open WebUI, and the OpenAI Python SDK via the base_url override.
Ollama loads one model per daemon instance. Switching models requires unloading one and loading another, which takes several seconds.
Ollama lacks vLLM's PagedAttention and continuous batching. It is optimized for a single interactive user, not hundreds of concurrent API consumers.
advanced settings like KV cache quantization, speculative decoding draft models, or custom CUDA kernel configurations require dropping down to llama.cpp directly.
very new models appear on Hugging Face in GGUF form before they appear in the Ollama library. You can import any GGUF file manually with a Modelfile, but the registry itself has a curation lag.
Ollama on Windows initially supported only CPU and DirectML. CUDA support on Windows was added in 2024; check the release notes for the current status on your platform.

When you run ollama serve (or any ollama command that requires a model), Ollama starts a Go background process that binds to localhost:11434. This process acts as both an HTTP server and a model manager. When a request arrives at /api/generate or /api/chat, the daemon checks whether the requested model is already loaded in memory. If it is, the request is dispatched immediately to the resident llama.cpp instance. If the model is not loaded, the daemon reads the GGUF file from disk into memory, sets up the llama.cpp context with the model's default or overridden parameters, and then dispatches the request. After a configurable idle timeout (default five minutes), the daemon unloads the model to free memory.

naming scheme, such as llama3.2:3b-instruct-q4_K_M or phi3:mini. The model name identifies the family and the tag specifies the variant — size, instruction-tuning status, and quantization level. Running ollama pull llama3.2 fetches the default tag (usually the smallest instruction-tuned variant). Specific tags are fetched explicitly. Tags correspond directly to GGUF files stored in Ollama's model registry, which is backed by object storage.

Locally, models are stored in ~/.ollama/models/ on Linux and macOS (and %APPDATA%\Ollama\models on Windows). The directory contains a blobs/ subdirectory with content-addressed GGUF files (shared across model variants that reuse the same base weights) and a manifests/ subdirectory with JSON manifests that describe which blobs compose each tagged model.
The diagram below shows how the Ollama daemon fits between clients and the llama.cpp backend, and the model pull workflow.

attrs.alt: Ollama Architecture diagram showing CLI/REST Client connecting to the Ollama Daemon, which uses llama.cpp backend for GPU/CPU inference, with model pull workflow from registry through GGUF download to local model storage

content.text: Ollama exposes three core inference endpoints under the /api/ path:
single-turn completion. Accepts a model name, a prompt string, optional system string, and generation parameters. Returns a streaming newline-delimited JSON response.
multi-turn conversation. Accepts an array of messages with role and content fields, matching the OpenAI messages format. Returns streamed tokens in the same format.
generates an embedding vector for the provided text using the specified model. Essential for RAG pipelines where document chunks must be indexed into a vector store.
Management endpoints include GET /api/tags (list installed models), DELETE /api/delete, POST /api/pull, and GET /api/show (model metadata including context length, parameter count, and Modelfile).

Ollama also maps to the OpenAI API format under /v1/: GET /v1/models returns a list in the OpenAI format, POST /v1/chat/completions works identically to the OpenAI chat completions endpoint, and POST /v1/embeddings follows the OpenAI embeddings format. This means any code written against the OpenAI Python SDK can switch to Ollama by changing only base_url.
Local development and prototyping where you want a model running in under two minutes with no cloud API key.
Privacy-sensitive applications that must not send data to an external API.
Offline or air-gapped environments where internet access is unavailable or restricted.
Single-user or low-concurrency use cases — an agent running in a developer's workflow, a personal assistant, a local code completion tool.
Applications where the model needs to be co-located with data for latency or compliance reasons.
Rapid evaluation of multiple models with the same application code, taking advantage of the OpenAI-compatible API.
Installing Ollama and Running a Model

# macOS / Linux (one-line installer)
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Windows: download the installer from https://ollama.com/download

# Pull and run a model interactively
ollama run llama3.2

# Pull without running (useful in CI or setup scripts)
ollama pull llama3.2:3b-instruct-q4_K_M

# List installed models
ollama list

# Show model details (architecture, quantization, context length)
ollama show llama3.2

# Remove a model to free disk space
ollama rm llama3.2

# Start the daemon explicitly (runs in foreground; usually started automatically)
ollama serve
Using the OpenAI SDK with Ollama

from openai import OpenAI

# Create a client pointing at the local Ollama server
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1",
    api_key="ollama",  # Any non-empty string works; Ollama ignores it
)

# Single-turn completion
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama3.2",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of Japan?"},
    ],
    temperature=0.1,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Streaming response
stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama3.2",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain transformers in three sentences."}],
    stream=True,
)
for chunk in stream:
    if chunk.choices[0].delta.content:
        print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content, end="", flush=True)
print()

# Embeddings
embedding_response = client.embeddings.create(
    model="nomic-embed-text",  # A dedicated embedding model in Ollama library
    input="The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog",
)
vector = embedding_response.data[0].embedding
print(f"Embedding dimension: {len(vector)}")
Modelfile for a Custom Agent Persona

# Save as Modelfile, then run: ollama create my-agent -f Modelfile
FROM llama3.2

# Set the system prompt permanently for this model variant
SYSTEM """
You are a senior software engineer specialized in Python and distributed systems.
When asked about code, always provide working examples with error handling.
Never suggest approaches you are not confident about.
If a question is outside your expertise, say so directly.
"""

# Override default generation parameters
PARAMETER temperature 0.1
PARAMETER top_p 0.9
PARAMETER num_ctx 16384
PARAMETER stop "<|eot_id|>"

# Build and run the custom model
# ollama create my-agent -f Modelfile
# ollama run my-agent

from langchain_ollama import ChatOllama, OllamaEmbeddings
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

# Chat model
llm = ChatOllama(
    model="llama3.2",
    temperature=0.1,
    base_url="http://localhost:11434",
)

messages = [
    SystemMessage(content="Answer concisely."),
    HumanMessage(content="What is PagedAttention?"),
]
response = llm.invoke(messages)
print(response.content)

# Embedding model for RAG
embeddings = OllamaEmbeddings(
    model="nomic-embed-text",
    base_url="http://localhost:11434",
)

# Build a local vector store from documents
docs = [
    "PagedAttention manages KV cache in fixed-size memory pages.",
    "Ollama wraps llama.cpp and provides a model library and REST API.",
    "GGUF is the file format used for quantized model weights.",
]
vectorstore = Chroma.from_texts(docs, embedding=embeddings)

# Retrieve relevant chunks and generate a response
query = "How does Ollama store models?"
retrieved = vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=2)
context = "
".join(d.page_content for d in retrieved)
rag_response = llm.invoke([
    HumanMessage(content=f"Context: {context}

Question: {query}")
])
print(rag_response.content)

A law firm wanted to let associates query a private document corpus — client memos, case precedents, and internal research notes — using natural language. Sending these documents to a cloud API was prohibited by client confidentiality policies. The team deployed Ollama on a Mac Studio M2 Ultra (192 GB unified memory) running llama3.1:70b-instruct-q4_K_M. The 70B model at Q4 quantization fit in approximately 45 GB of unified memory, generating 20–30 tokens per second on the Metal backend — fast enough for interactive use.

They built a RAG pipeline using LangChain's ChatOllama and OllamaEmbeddings (backed by nomic-embed-text), indexing documents into a Chroma vector store on the same machine. Associates typed questions into a simple web UI built with Open WebUI, which communicates with Ollama's OpenAI-compatible endpoint. The entire pipeline — document ingestion, embedding, retrieval, and generation — ran on a single Mac with no cloud services and no data leaving the building. Total infrastructure cost was the Mac Studio hardware and a one-time setup day, against the ongoing cost of cloud API calls that would have run to several thousand dollars per month at the query volume the firm needed.
Not pulling an embedding model separately:
the chat model and the embedding model are different. Running ollama pull llama3.2 does not install an embedding model. Use ollama pull nomic-embed-text or mxbai-embed-large for RAG pipelines.

Ollama runs on port 11434, not 8080 or 11435. The base_url for OpenAI-compat usage is http://localhost:11434/v1, not http://localhost:11434 alone (the /v1 prefix is required for the OpenAI-compatible endpoints).
Running ollama serve when the daemon is already running:
Ollama auto-starts the daemon when needed. Running ollama serve manually after installation usually results in a 'port already in use' error. Check ollama ps first.

Ollama's default num_ctx is 2048 for many models. If your prompt plus expected completion is longer than this, set PARAMETER num_ctx in a Modelfile or pass num_ctx in the API request options. Exceeding the context silently truncates input.
Expecting multi-user throughput comparable to a dedicated GPU server:

Ollama is optimized for a single user on a developer machine. Under concurrent load from multiple users, response latency degrades because the engine is not designed for continuous batching across many parallel sequences.
capture system prompts and parameter overrides in a Modelfile and commit it to your project repository. This ensures every team member runs the same model configuration.
use the full tag (llama3.2:3b-instruct-q4_K_M) in production scripts rather than the default tag. Default tags can be reassigned to newer versions in the registry.
if your test suite calls a local Ollama server, add an ollama pull step to your CI configuration so the model is available before tests run.

the environment variable OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE controls how long Ollama keeps a model in memory after the last request. The default is five minutes. Set it to -1 (keep forever) in server deployments, or 0 (unload immediately) on memory-constrained devices.
Use the native API for streaming:

Ollama's native /api/generate endpoint is simpler and slightly more efficient than the OpenAI-compat endpoint for single-turn generation. Use the OpenAI-compat endpoint only when framework compatibility is needed.

ollama ps shows which models are currently loaded, their GPU/CPU memory usage, and their idle timeout. Use it to diagnose why inference is slow (model unloaded) or memory is high (too many models cached).

On Apple Silicon, Ollama's Metal backend achieves approximately 40–80 tokens per second on a Q4 7B model on an M3 Pro, and 20–40 tokens per second on the Q4 70B model on an M2 Ultra. The unified memory architecture is a significant advantage: the GPU and CPU share the same memory pool, so there is no PCIe bottleneck when the model is too large for a discrete GPU's VRAM.

On NVIDIA hardware, Ollama with CUDA achieves throughput similar to a bare llama.cpp server with CUDA enabled. A single RTX 4090 (24 GB) running a Q4 8B model generates 120–150 tokens per second. For workloads needing higher throughput or true multi-user serving, vLLM with PagedAttention and continuous batching will substantially outperform Ollama at the cost of more complex deployment.

The OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL environment variable (default 1) controls how many requests Ollama processes simultaneously. Increasing it to 4 or 8 can improve aggregate throughput for small batches of concurrent requests but increases memory usage and individual request latency.
a self-hosted web interface compatible with Ollama and OpenAI APIs, providing a ChatGPT-style UI for local models with history, RAG support, and model switching.
the langchain_ollama package provides ChatOllama and OllamaEmbeddings classes that integrate with the full LangChain ecosystem.
the llama-index-llms-ollama and llama-index-embeddings-ollama packages connect Ollama to LlamaIndex query engines and RAG pipelines.
a VS Code and JetBrains extension that uses Ollama as a code completion and chat backend directly inside the IDE.
a CLI tool that uses Ollama or OpenAI to run prompt patterns for summarization, extraction, and structured writing tasks.
Podman AI Lab / Docker Model Runner:
container runtimes have added native LLM serving features that integrate with Ollama-compatible APIs, making local models composable with containerized services.

LM Studio is the closest competitor to Ollama in terms of audience, but it prioritizes a graphical model browser over scriptability. Jan offers a similar desktop experience with a focus on user privacy and an extensible plugin architecture. GPT4All targets non-technical users with a fully self-contained installer but has historically supported fewer model families and offered less API flexibility. For programmatic local LLM access, Ollama is generally the best choice due to its clean API, active model registry, and widespread framework integration.

Ollama binds to 127.0.0.1:11434 by default. If you set OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 to allow network access from other machines, add firewall rules or a reverse proxy with authentication, as there is no built-in auth mechanism.
models in the official Ollama registry are SHA-256 verified during pull. When importing GGUF files manually via a Modelfile FROM instruction, verify the file hash against the source before loading.

if a conversation grows beyond the model's num_ctx setting, Ollama silently truncates older messages from the beginning of the context. For long-running agentic sessions, monitor total token usage and implement sliding window or summarization strategies at the application layer.
Ollama runs as a user-level process with access to the file system. Do not point Ollama at untrusted GGUF files from unknown sources, as the weight loader reads arbitrary binary data.
Ollama applies no content filtering beyond what is baked into the model's training. Applications are responsible for validating and filtering model outputs before presenting them to end users.
The most useful mental model for Ollama is

. In Docker, you pull an image by name, run it as a container, and interact with the service it exposes through a port. In Ollama, you pull a model by name, run it (the daemon process is the container), and interact through port 11434. A Modelfile plays the role of a Dockerfile: it specifies the base model and the customizations applied on top. The Ollama library is Docker Hub. The GGUF file inside ~/.ollama/models is the image layer. When the container is idle, it can be stopped to free resources; when a request arrives, it starts up again. The OpenAI-compatible API is the stable interface contract, the equivalent of the HTTP protocol that makes containers composable regardless of what's inside them.

This mental model also clarifies what Ollama is not: it is not a training tool, not a model evaluation harness, and not a high-throughput production inference server. It is the runtime layer that makes a specific model available to an application with minimum friction, the same way Docker makes a specific service available to a developer environment without requiring manual installation of its dependencies.
Ollama reduces local LLM setup to two commands — ollama pull and ollama run — by managing model downloads, format conversion, and hardware acceleration automatically.
The daemon process keeps models loaded in memory across requests, eliminating cold-start latency for interactive applications.
The OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions and /v1/embeddings endpoints mean existing LangChain, LlamaIndex, and OpenAI SDK code switches to Ollama by changing only the base_url.
Modelfiles version system prompts and generation parameters in a Dockerfile-like format, making model configuration reproducible and shareable.
Ollama is optimized for a single interactive user or low-concurrency workloads; for high-throughput multi-user serving, vLLM or TGI are more appropriate.
On Apple Silicon, Ollama's Metal backend delivers strong tokens-per-second rates on models that fit in unified memory, making M-series Macs practical inference machines for 7B to 70B models.
The correct mental model is Docker for LLMs: pull a model, run it through a daemon, interact via a REST API, and customize with a Modelfile.
